coredata.dc:title: A gradient photothermal hydrogel with carbon-dots deposited molybdenum disulfide nanoflowers as photothermal centers for efficient solar-driven water evaporation and treatment
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jinqing
Li, Can
Chen, Lin
Li, Tao
Gao, Fucheng
Chen, Xintong
Zhao, Tingting
Wang, Fenglong
Jiang, Yanyan
coredata.dc:description: Given the urgency of water pollution and freshwater scarcity, the pursuit of efficient and clean desalination techniques has gained prominence. In this study, a multi-defective molybdenum disulfide nanoflower was synthesized within a carbon-dots solution environment, named CDs/MoS2, exhibiting exceptional photothermal and catalytic properties. Relying on the excellent photothermal properties, antibacterial rates of up to 94% and 99% against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli. Additionally, the CDs/MoS2 nanoflowers exhibited a remarkable removal efficiency of 91% for high concentrations of methylene blue (MB) through photodegradation. A gradient photothermal composite hydrogel (CDs/MoS2@PVA Hydrogel) was designed to serve as a solar-driven clean water generator to isolate drinking water from various non-potable water. The gradient hydrogel effectively enhanced solar energy absorption through light trapping, creating dispersed hot spots to capture and activate water molecules. As a result, it achieved a high evaporation rate of 1.49 kg m-2 h-1 at a solar radiation of 1 kW m-2, with an impressive solar-to-vapor conversion efficiency of 92.5%. The hydrogel was successfully employed in diverse water treatment applications, including desalination, anti-bacteria and removal of organic contaminants from water. This study presents an innovative approach to developing novel solar vapor materials for water purification purposes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solar-driven interfacial evaporation
Molybdenum disulfide
Carbon-dots
Hydrogels
Desalination
Dye degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photothermal performance of the CDs/MoS2 nanoflowers
Photothermal antimicrobial properties of CDs/MoS2 nanoflowers
Photocatalytic dye degradation performance of the CDs/MoS2 nanoflowers
Solar-powered water evaporation performance and water purification performance of the CMPH

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The synthetic process of CDs/MoS2 by hydrothermal method. (b) XRD patterns of CDs, MoS2, CDs/MoS2. (c) TEM image of CDs. (d) SEM image of MoS2. (e) SEM image of CDs/MoS2. (f) TEM image of CDs/MoS2. (g) HRTEM image of CDs/MoS2.

(a) HRTEM image of CDs/MoS2. (b) Corresponding FFT patterns of CDs/MoS2. (c) Corresponding IFFT patterns of CDs/MoS2. (d) Element mapping of CDs/MoS2. (e) FTIR spectrum of CDs, MoS2 and CDs/MoS2. (f) XPS survey spectrum of MoS2 and CDs/MoS2. (g) Mo 3d spectra, (h) S 2p spectra and (i) C 1s spectra of CDs/MoS2.

(a-c) Temperature elevation of water, CDs (a), MoS2 (b) and CDs/MoS2 (c) at different concentrations under NIR laser power densities (808 nm, 1 W/cm2). (d) Temperature elevation of CDs/MoS2 under different laser power densities (0.5,1,1.5,2 W/cm2) upon 808 nm. (e-f) Temperature variation (e) diagram and (f) photo of H2O, CDs, MoS2 and CDs/MoS2 at a concentration of 1 mg/mL under irradiation (808 nm, 1 W/cm2). (g) Temperature elevation curve of 1mg/mL CDs/MoS2 in four cycles of 808 nm NIR later on/off irradiation under 1 W/cm2 laser power densities. (h) The photothermal conversion efficiency and heating-cooling curve of CDs/MoS2. (i) UV–vis-NIR absorption spectra of CDs/MoS2.
(a) and (b) are the photographs and viability of S. aureus colonies, respectively. (c) and (d) are the photographs and viability of E. coli colonies, respectively.

(a) Photocatalytic mechanism diagram of CDs/MoS2. (b) Blank experiments. (c) Photo-degradation rates of 50 mL 200 ppm MB solution with different dosages of CDs/MoS2 powder and 2 mL 30 wt% H2O2, and corresponding. (d) Photo-degradation rate curves. (e) Ultraviolet absorption spectra of MB solutions with 80 mg CDs/MoS2 powder and 2 mL H2O2 at different time intervals. (f) Photo-degradation rates of 50 mL 200 ppm MB solution with MoS2 (80 mg) and CDs/MoS2 (80 mg) in the presence of 2 mL 30 wt% H2O2 under 1-sun irradiation, and corresponding (g) Kinetic rate constants of the MB degradation for different samples (MoS2, CDs/MoS2).

(a) The schematic diagram of the solar-powered water evaporation measuring device. (b) Time-dependent mass variation of the solar-driven water evaporation, and (c) evaporation rate of water, PVAH, MPH, and CMPH. (d) IR thermal images of wet PVAH, MPH and CMPH under 1-sun downward irradiation, and corresponding original digital pictures. (e) Temperature curves of water, dry PVAH, MPH and CMPH in response to the light irradiation. (f) Concentrations of different ions in actual seawater and evaporated water (each experiment was repeated for three times). Absorption spectra of (g) methyl orange solution and (h) methylene blue solution before and after the solar steam generation treatment with the CMPH device under 1-sun irradiation. The insets are digital pictures of the solutions before and after the purification.

head.ce:title: A gradient photothermal hydrogel with carbon-dots deposited molybdenum disulfide nanoflowers as photothermal centers for efficient solar-driven water evaporation and treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Liquid-Solid Structural Evolution and Processing of Materials, Ministry of Education, Shandong University, Jinan, 250061, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Liquid-Solid Structural Evolution and Processing of Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aKey Laboratory for Liquid-Solid Structural Evolution and Processing of Materials, Ministry of Education, Shandong University, Jinan, 250061, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Haide College, Ocean University of China, Qingdao, 266100, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bHaide College, Ocean University of China, Qingdao, 266100, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Key Laboratory of Advanced Aluminium Materials and Technology, Binzhou Institute of Technology, Weiqiao-UCAS Science and Technology Park, Binzhou, 256606, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Key Laboratory of Advanced Aluminium Materials and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cShandong Key Laboratory of Advanced Aluminium Materials and Technology, Binzhou Institute of Technology, Weiqiao-UCAS Science and Technology Park, Binzhou, 256606, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Given the urgency of water pollution and freshwater scarcity, the pursuit of efficient and clean desalination techniques has gained prominence. In this study, a multi-defective molybdenum disulfide nanoflower was synthesized within a carbon-dots solution environment, named CDs/MoS2, exhibiting exceptional photothermal and catalytic properties. Relying on the excellent photothermal properties, antibacterial rates of up to 94% and 99% against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli. Additionally, the CDs/MoS2 nanoflowers exhibited a remarkable removal efficiency of 91% for high concentrations of methylene blue (MB) through photodegradation. A gradient photothermal composite hydrogel (CDs/MoS2@PVA Hydrogel) was designed to serve as a solar-driven clean water generator to isolate drinking water from various non-potable water. The gradient hydrogel effectively enhanced solar energy absorption through light trapping, creating dispersed hot spots to capture and activate water molecules. As a result, it achieved a high evaporation rate of 1.49 kg m-2 h-1 at a solar radiation of 1 kW m-2, with an impressive solar-to-vapor conversion efficiency of 92.5%. The hydrogel was successfully employed in diverse water treatment applications, including desalination, anti-bacteria and removal of organic contaminants from water. This study presents an innovative approach to developing novel solar vapor materials for water purification purposes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution and the increasing shortage of freshwater resources have become urgent problems due to the tremendous pressure exerted by the rapid development of modern society and the continuous growth of the global population [1]. In this situation, due to the abundance of seawater resources, efficient and clean desalination technology may gradually become an important solution for obtaining fresh water [2–7]. The emerging technology of interfacial seawater evaporation presents a promising method for enhancing solar energy utilization efficiency. By concentrating solar energy at the interface of water and air, this approach effectively localizes heat generation on the surface of the evaporator. Consequently, it significantly reduces energy loss through heat conduction into the water and minimizes longitudinal heat loss from the evaporator. This strategic concentration of energy thus optimizes the overall efficiency of the system. [8–12]. Various photothermal materials, such as carbon materials [13–15], metal nanoparticles [16,17], and semiconductor materials [18,19], have been developed and utilized in this field. For instance, utilizing carbon nanotubes (CNTs) as a photothermal material in the production of carbon-based hydrogels led to an evaporator with an exceptionally high evaporation rate of 16.35 kg m-2h-1 under 1 sunlight and a 3 A current [20]. Consequently, To further enhance water evaporation efficiencies, it is essential to continuously advance the design and development of photothermal materials and evaporator systems with high conversion efficiencies.

Recent research has indicated that MoS2, with its narrow bandgap (≈1.2 eV), exhibits exceptional physicochemical properties [21], enabling high absorption and efficient photothermal conversion over a wide spectral range [22]. This material also displays significant edge activity [23], facilitating the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants by generating hole/electron pairs upon exposure to light [24]. The distinctive characteristics of MoS2 make it a highly promising candidate for applications in photothermal water evaporation and wastewater purification. However, several challenges impede its practical applicability, including the suboptimal photothermal conversion efficiency and the limited water dispersibility. To address these limitations, recent research studies have proposed enhancing the performance of MoS2 by immobilizing various organic molecules [25,26] and inorganic nanoparticles [27,28] onto the nanosheet surface through chemical modification or physical adsorption. Meanwhile, carbon-dots (CDs), a novel class of zero-dimensional nanomaterials characterized by a carbon core surrounded by a polymer shell with diverse functional groups [29], offer distinct advantages, including excellent water solubility, a large specific surface area, and numerous active sites [30]. Emerging investigations have shown that CDs not only promote nanoparticle synthesis but also augment catalytic activity [31–36], underscoring their potential in advanced applications. The polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) hydrogel backbone is characterized by a high concentration of hydroxyl groups, which play a crucial role in activating native or free water. This activation facilitates the formation of a substantial amount of intermediate water, which is essential for rapid evaporation under natural sunlight. For instance, Lu and colleagues used various photothermal materials to phase-engineer PVA through physical and chemical cross-linking, achieving an energy efficiency of 98.0% and a record evaporation rate of approximately 4.45 kg m-2 h-1[37,38]. Consequently, PVA hydrogels have proven effective in solar-driven interfacial evaporation processes, significantly enhancing solar water purification performance.

In this study, the in situ synthesis of molybdenum disulfide nanoflowers was successfully achieved through a hydrothermal reaction in a high concentration of carbon-dots to obtain CDs/MoS2. The CDs/MoS2 underwent significant changes in structure and morphology compared to pristine MoS2. What's more, the photothermal efficiency and catalytic activity of the CDs/MoS2 were substantially enhanced. Leveraging the unique properties of CDs/MoS2, we integrated these materials with poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) hydrogel to develop an innovative asymmetric hydrogel evaporator. This was achieved by employing a repeated freezing and thawing process, which enriched the photo-thermal materials on one side of the hydrogel while minimizing channel blockage within its structure. The resulting structure enabled effective solar water evaporation and photocatalytic wastewater treatment under sunlight. This approach not only facilitated remarkable desalination in wastewater treatment but also demonstrated excellent efficiency in the photodegradation and removal of high concentrations of methylene blue (MB). This research expands the current understanding of nanomaterial design with improved photothermal and photocatalytic capabilities, with a specific focus on materials synthesis for advanced water purification.

The synthesis mechanism of CDs/MoS2 nanoflowers, illustrated in Fig. 1 a, involves a multi-step process. Initially, carbon dots (CDs) are prepared through a hydrothermal reaction, following a previously established method [39]. Subsequently, molybdenum and sulfur precursors are directly introduced into the solution. This is followed by the formation of the composite material, which is achieved using a one-pot hydrothermal method.

The successful preparation of CDs was confirmed through various analyses. X-ray diffraction (XRD)patterns (Fig. 1b) indicated the presence of CDs in the form of amorphous carbon [40]. This was further supported by the atomic lattice spacing of 0.21 nm as revealed by TEM, which aligned with the (100) facet of graphitic carbon (Fig. 1c) [29]. It was noted that some CDs exhibited a pronounced tendency to agglomerate. In addition, other relevant properties were presented in Figure S1. Importantly, the XRD pattern validated the production of CDs/MoS2 (Fig. 1b), with characteristic diffraction peaks at 32.9°, 35.9°, and 58.7°, corresponding to the (100), (102), and (110) crystallographic planes of standard hexagonal MoS2 (standardized card PDF 75–1539) [41]. However, the absence of the diffraction peak at 14.1° corresponding to the (002) crystal face in CDs/MoS2 compared to MoS2 implied a reduced level of crystallization, supported by existing literature [42,43]. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Fig. 1e and f) illustrated the morphology of the CDs/MoS2 nanoflowers, which was approximately 900 nm in size and exhibited distinct changes compared to the compact structure of MoS2 (Fig. 1d).High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) analysis (Fig. 1g) revealed the atomic crystal plane spacing on the (002) crystal plane of CDs/MoS2 to be 0.62 nm [44]. However, the CDs were not visually apparent in the image, which might be sheltered by MoS2.

To investigate the existence of the CDs, an enlarged observation of the MoS2 partial surface was conducted (Fig. 2 a). Even though no visible CDs were detected on the sample surface, it displayed a fuzzy appearance with short-range ordering. The surface image was subjected to Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) to generate a circular ring image (Fig. 2b), indicating the amorphous state of the CDs/MoS2 composites. Subsequent analysis via Inverse Fast Fourier Transform (IFFT) revealed numerous dislocations on the surface of the material (Fig. 2c), suggesting the influence of CDs doping. Notably, the similarity between the lattice fringes of CDs and the 0.23 nm lattice fringes on the MoS2 (100) crystal surface suggested that the presence of dislocation defects could be attributed to CDs doping. For verification of the presence of CDs, EDX spectroscopy revealed an even distribution of C elements with a content of up to 36%, further verifying the presence of CDs (Fig. 2d and S2). FT-IR analysis was conducted to examine the functional groups in each sample. The bands at 2863 and 2921 cm-1 in the black traces corresponding to CDs were associated with C-H stretching vibrations (Fig. 2e). The FT-IR spectra of CDs/MoS2 displayed similarities to those of MoS2, suggesting the in situ synthesis strategy in the presence of CDs had minimal impact on the composition of the functional groups of the MoS2 surface. Furthermore, characteristic peaks at 3450, 1620, and 1385 cm-1 corresponded to O-H, C=O, and C=C functional groups, while vibrations at 1112 and 658 cm-1 were attributed to C-OH and C-S stretching, respectively [32,45]. Analysis through XPS confirmed the presence of elements C, N, O, S, and Mo in the CDs/MoS2 composites, consistent with EDS results (Fig. 2f). The prominent C 1s peak reaffirmed the high concentration of carbon in the composite. The CDs/MoS2 Mo 3d diagram (Fig. 2g) highlighted typical Mo-S bonding at 232.6 eV and 229.5 eV for the Mo 3d3/2 and Mo 3d5/2 features [46]. The S 2p high-resolution XPS spectra depicted S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 features (Fig. 2h), while a detailed analysis of the Mo 3d and C 1s high-resolution XPS spectra indicated that CDs were not chemically bonded to MoS2 but existed independently (Fig. 2i). Overall, the analysis led to the hypothesis that CDs essentially acted as nuclei during the reaction and doped into MoS2. This process facilitated the synthesis of MoS2 and at the same time induced surface defects, enhancing the dispersibility and catalytic activity of the resulted nanomaterial in aqueous environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal performance of the CDs/MoS2 nanoflowers
ce:section.ce:para: An infrared camera was employed to assess the photothermal performance in the near-infrared (NIR) region and determine photothermal conversion efficiencies. The photothermal properties of CDs/MoS2 nanoflowers were impressive, as demonstrated (Fig. 3 a-d) by the significant temperature changes under monochromatic 808 nm laser irradiation when comparing with CDs, MoS2 and CDs/MoS2. The warming trend of CDs/MoS2 became increasingly pronounced with higher concentrations, resulting in an evident elevation in the maximum temperature achieved. Notably, the temperature of the 1 mg/mL CDs/MoS2 solution increased by 44.5 °C, highlighting its exceptional photothermal performance. Moreover, the temperature variation at different laser power densities demonstrated a positive correlation between the maximum temperature and power density, indicating that higher optical power densities led to higher temperatures (Fig. 3e-3f). To assess the photothermal stability, a 1 mg/mL CDs/MoS2 solution was subjected to four on/off cycle experiments with an 808 nm laser lamp at a power density of 1 W/cm2 (Fig. 3g). The experiments consistently maintained stable temperature profiles, highlighting the reliable photothermal conversion properties of CDs/MoS2. The temperature profiles of CDs/MoS2 solutions demonstrate photothermal conversion efficiencies of up to 55%, significantly higher than those of MoS2 (38.4%) and CDs (16.6%) (Fig. 3h, Figure S3). Fig. 3i demonstrated that the CDs/MoS2 efficiently absorbed a broad range of the solar spectrum, spanning from 300 to 2400 nm, with absorption efficiencies exceeding 95%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal antimicrobial properties of CDs/MoS2 nanoflowers
ce:section.ce:para: In investigating the photothermal antimicrobial properties of the materials, S. aureus and E. coli were co-incubated with the materials for 10 minutes under the irradiation of NIR light at 808 nm. The results, as shown in Fig. 4 , were obtained from control experiments conducted with Blank, MoS2, and CDs/MoS2, both with and without NIR light irradiation. Figs. 4a and b present the outcomes for S. aureus, while Figs. 4c and d illustrate the results for E. coli. It was observed that the survival rates of bacteria under NIR irradiation were high at 94.8% and 88%, respectively, indicating that the irradiation did not have a toxic effect on the cells of S. aureus and E. coli. On the other hand, MoS2 and CDs/MoS2 alone showed antibacterial effects, likely because of the release of Mo4+ from polycrystalline MoS2 in the water solution, which is known to have bactericidal properties [47]. Notably, CDs/MoS2 exhibited the highest antibacterial activity among all groups, with antimicrobial rates as high as 94% (Fig. 4b) and 99% (Fig. 4d). The findings suggest that the materials effectively induced bacterial death, likely attributable to the photothermal properties of the materials under NIR radiation. Previous studies have highlighted that the photothermal effect can cause severe damage to bacterial cell membranes, thereby impeding bacterial growth [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic dye degradation performance of the CDs/MoS2 nanoflowers
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs/MoS2 nanoflowers exhibit a significantly enhanced rate of photodegradation for the dye contaminant in wastewater, highlighting their significant potential for freshwater reclamation from industrial wastewater. The process of photodegradation for organic matter is driven by sunlight, where photo-excited reduction and oxidation reactions of electrons and holes lead to the generation of reactive oxide species (ROS). Through a photocatalytic process, CDs/MoS2 could effectively degrade organic pollutants into benign carbon dioxide and water through the illustrated mechanism in Fig. 5 a. To facilitate this investigation, a 30 wt% concentration of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was chosen as an effective and environmentally friendly oxidizing agent. Furthermore, the organic dye MB used as a representative water pollutant to assess the photocatalytic activity of CDs/MoS2 when exposed to sunlight irradiation.

The catalytic process was evaluated by stirring the catalyst powder and collecting dye solutions at various time intervals to measure UV–visible absorption. The control experiment demonstrated that the concentration of pure MB solution scarcely decreased after 2 hours of 1-sun irradiation, indicating the stability of MB, as depicted in Fig. 5b. Additionally, it was observed that the concentration of MB solution remained unchanged when only pure MoS2, pure H2O2, and pure CDs/MoS2 were added, suggesting that the introduction of a single component would not impact the photocatalytic outcomes. In the CDs/MoS2/H2O2/light system, the MB degradation rate increased from 3.21% to 91.39% with an increase in CDs/MoS2 catalyst dosage, demonstrating the effectiveness of the system in dye removal (Fig. 5c). In addition, it was found that further increasing the CDs/MoS2 dosage to 100 mg resulted in minimal change in the degradation rate of MB, indicating that 80 mg of CDs/MoS2 was the optimum dosage for the CDs/MoS2/H2O2/light system. This optimum dosage was confirmed by analyzing the MB concentration curves over time for different catalyst dosages (Fig. 5d-5e). After two hours, the absorption peak at 665 nm of the MB solution almost disappeared, indicating the successful degradation of MB. In comparison with the MoS2/H2O2/light system showed, the CDs/MoS2/H2O2/light system exhibited a remarkable improvement in MB degradation efficiency, reaching as high as 91% (Fig. 5f). By calculating the photocatalytic activity of these two composite systems, the rate constants of MB degradation were determined to be 0.02038 min-1, illustrating the efficacy of the CDs/MoS2 composite catalysts (Fig. 5g). These findings indicate that MoS2 plays a crucial role as the primary active component in activating H2O2, displaying excellent photodegradation performance. Furthermore, the presence of CDs in the CDs/MoS2 composites enhances the photocatalytic performance of the system, underscoring the synergistic effect of the composite catalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solar-powered water evaporation performance and water purification performance of the CMPH
ce:section.ce:para: The exceptional combination of high light absorption and conversion efficiency of CDs/MoS2 in the full spectrum (Fig. 3i) suggested its outstanding performance in solar-powered water evaporation. The conception of interfacial evaporation, a highly effective approach for reducing heat loss and promoting extensive light absorption by converting solar energy into thermal energy at gas-liquid interfaces, served as inspiration for incorporating the CDs/MoS2 nanoflowers into PVA to obtain the CMPH. In practical applications, light-absorbing hydrogels are often required to have a porous structure to facilitate evaporation. However, this structural characteristic typically results in relatively low mechanical properties. To effectively function in harsh environments, such as those with high salinity, wind waves, and thermal cycling, these hydrogels must also possess robust mechanical properties. Therefore, balancing the need for a porous structure with the requirement for enhanced mechanical strength is crucial for the successful application of hydrogels in such challenging conditions. The incorporation of CDs/MoS2 into PVAH was demonstrated in Figure S4, and the inclusion of CDs/MoS2 significantly enhanced the mechanical properties of PVAH, as shown in Figure S5. During the slow gelation process, CDs/MoS2 nanoflowers naturally settled on the bottom of the mold, creating a PVAH water evaporator with an asymmetric distribution of photothermal material on the top and bottom surfaces (Figure S6). During water evaporation, the higher concentration on the lower bottom surface is converted to the upper surface in order to achieve heat concentration on the evaporator surface, and reduce the heat loss when water is transferred upwards from the lower end of the evaporator, resulting in improving thermal management. This innovation aims to enhance the photothermal conversion efficiency and overall performance of solar vaporizers used for solar-driven interfacial evaporation.

To assess the efficiency of solar-driven water evaporation, a test rig was constructed to evaluate the performance of solar vapor generation. Fig. 6 a presents a schematic diagram of the solar-powered water evaporation measuring device. This apparatus employed an AM 1.5G xenon lamp to simulate sunlight irradiation, an electronic balance to monitor changes in water mass, and an infrared thermography camera to capture real-time temperature variations on the hydrogel surface. Under simulated sunlight with an optical power density of 0.1 W/cm² (equivalent to 1 sun), a significant amount of water vapor was observed forming on the surface of the floating CMPH. Infrared thermal images indicated a marked temperature increase at the water-air interface of the CMPH, with the surface temperature of the CMPH being notably higher than that of the surrounding water, which remained at room temperature (Figure S6). To further investigate the solar water evaporation rate and solar-vapor conversion efficiency, the temperature of the hydrogel surface and the mass change of water were recorded over time under solar radiation (1 sun, 1 kW m-2). The mass of water exhibited a gradual decrease, following a linear trend as the duration of solar irradiation increased, with the slope of the curve stabilizing over time (Fig. 6b). By analyzing the slopes of the mass change curves, the evaporation rates for different samples were calculated (Fig. 6c). The evaporation efficiency (η) was determined using the following equation:
η =
m h
C
o p t
P 0
where m represents the net evaporation rate after deducting the evaporation rate of the sponge with the top surface covered under ambient light, P0 stands for the solar irradiation power (1 kW/m2 in this study), Copt denotes the optical concentration and h represents the evaporation enthalpy of the water in the porous sponge. As a result, MPH and PVAH exhibited the stabilized evaporation rates of 0.92 kg m-2 h-1 and 0.42 kg m-2 h-1, respectively. In comparison, the evaporation rate of CMPH stabilized significantly at 1.49 kg m⁻² h⁻¹, which is 4.2 times higher than that of water under identical conditions. This remarkable performance is accompanied by an impressive solar-to-vapor conversion efficiency of 92.5%. Such efficiency and evaporation rates are prominently highlighted in much of the previously reported research on MoS₂-based evaporators. (Table S1, Supplementary Information). As depicted in Fig. 6d, the thermal localization effect at the evaporation interface led to an increase in the surface temperature of CMPH from room temperature to a maximum of 42.3 °C within approximately 15 minutes, while the surfaces of MPH and PVAH reached a lower equilibrium temperature of within a longer time of 60 minutes. This confirmed the excellent energy conversion and photothermal evaporation capability of CMPH, making it a promising evaporator for solar thermal evaporation, as also demonstrated in the infrared images of CMPH, MPH, and PVAH at different time intervals in Fig. 6d-6e.

The CMPH evaporator displayed remarkable water purification efficiency. Desalination experiments were performed using seawater samples from the Yellow Sea, and the resulting water vapor was analyzed using ICP-OES, as shown in Fig. 6f. The analysis revealed a notable decrease in the concentrations of the four major ions (Na+, Mg2+, K+, and Ca2+) post-treatment, resulting in fresh water with the salinity meeting the drinking water standards set by the World Health Organization (WHO) [49]. Additionally, to prove their potential for dye removal, deionized water contaminated with methyl orange and methylene blue dyes, mimicking wastewater was subjected to solar evaporation. The MO (∼465 nm) and MB (∼665 nm) absorption peaks in the purified water, as illustrated in Fig. 6g and h, demonstrated the simulating wastewater effectiveness of the evaporator [50]. These outcomes underscore the exceptional water purification capabilities of the CMPH evaporator, indicating its potential as a promising solution for solar water purification.

In summary, this study successfully synthesized defect-rich CDs/MoS2 nanoflowers by modifying the reaction solution. The resulting material demonstrated exceptional photothermal and catalytic properties, including a photothermal conversion efficiency of up to 55%. It achieved antibacterial rates of 99% and 94% against S. aureus and E. coli, respectively, and was capable of degrading over 91.39% of MB dye in wastewater. Notably, the material exhibited a high light absorption rate exceeding 95% across a broad spectral range, particularly within the solar irradiation spectrum. Subsequently, a natural deposition method was employed to develop a gradient-type CDs/MoS2/PVA hydrogel. This innovative design effectively concentrated heat on the upper surface of the hydrogel while minimizing channel blockage within its structure. Under one sun's irradiation, the hydrogel demonstrated an evaporation efficiency of 1.49 kg m-2 h-1 and a conversion efficiency of 92.5%, presenting a promising strategy for comprehensive water purification using hydrogel technology. This study provides valuable insights into advancing nanomaterials with enhanced photothermal and photocatalytic capabilities, highlighting their potential applications in wastewater treatment.

Jinqing Li: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Can Li: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Lin Chen: Data curation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Tao Li: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology, Data curation. Fucheng Gao: Visualization, Methodology. Xintong Chen: Methodology, Conceptualization. Tingting Zhao: Validation. Fenglong Wang: Methodology, Investigation. Yanyan Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no competing interests to declare

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52101287) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant No. 2022JC002, 2022JC020).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.surfin.2024.105433.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Global water shortage and potable water safety; today's concern and tomorrow's crisis
Environ. Int.
